name: Amines
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Structure, Classification and Nomenclature
sections.body:
Amines are derivatives of NH3 in which one, two, or three H atoms are replaced by alkyl/aryl groups. The N atom is sp³ hybridised with a lone pair, giving a pyramidal shape.

Classification:

  • Primary (1°): R–NH2 (one H replaced).
  • Secondary (2°): R2NH (two H replaced).
  • Tertiary (3°): R3N (all three H replaced).

Quaternary ammonium: R4N⁺ X⁻.

Nomenclature:

  • Common: alkyl group(s) + amine. CH3NH2 = methylamine; (CH3)2NH = dimethylamine; (CH3)3N = trimethylamine.
  • IUPAC: N-alkyl prefix to the parent amine (alkanamine). CH3NH2 = methanamine; CH3NHCH2CH3 = N-methylethanamine; C6H5NH2 = aniline (retained) or benzenamine; C6H5N(CH3)2 = N,N-dimethylaniline.

sections.heading: Methods of Preparation
sections.body:
Reduction of nitro compounds: Ar–NO2 →Sn/HCl or Fe/HCl or H2/Ni Ar–NH2. Used for aniline (from nitrobenzene).

Ammonolysis of alkyl halides: R–X + NH3 (excess, sealed tube, heat) → R–NH2 + HX. Gives a mixture of 1°, 2°, 3° amines and quaternary salt because the products are also nucleophilic. Reactivity: R–I > R–Br > R–Cl. Excess ammonia favours 1° amine.

Reduction of nitriles: R–CN + 4[H] (LiAlH4 or H2/Ni) → R–CH2–NH2 (1° amine, one C added). Useful for chain extension.

Reduction of amides: R–CONH2 + LiAlH4 → R–CH2–NH2 (1° amine, no C added).

Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: gives pure 1° amine only. Phthalimide → potassium phthalimide (KOH) → R–phthalimide (R–X) → R–NH2 + phthalic acid (NaOH or NH2NH2). Cannot make aromatic primary amines because Ar–X doesn't react.

Hofmann bromamide degradation: R–CONH2 + Br2 + 4 NaOH → R–NH2 + Na2CO3 + 2 NaBr. Loses one C atom; gives only 1° amine. Mechanism via isocyanate (R–N=C=O) intermediate.

sections.heading: Physical Properties
sections.body:
Lower aliphatic amines are gases with fishy smell; higher ones are liquids/solids. Aniline is a colourless oil that turns brown on standing in air (oxidation).

Boiling points: 1° and 2° amines form intermolecular H-bonds (N–H···N); 3° amines cannot. Order at similar mass: 1° > 2° > 3° amine. Amines have higher b.p. than alkanes/ethers but lower than alcohols (N–H···N is weaker than O–H···O, since N is less electronegative than O).

Solubility: lower amines are soluble in water (H-bond with water); solubility decreases with chain length. Aniline is sparingly soluble.

sections.heading: Basicity of Amines
sections.body:
Amines are basic (lone pair on N accepts H⁺). Basicity ≡ stability of R3NH⁺ ↔ ease of accepting H⁺.

In gas phase (no solvation): basicity follows +I effect of alkyl groups: 3° > 2° > 1° > NH3.

In aqueous solution (Kb), the order is more nuanced because of solvation of cation:

  • Aliphatic amines (in water): generally 2° > 1° > 3° > NH3 for methylamines. For ethyl: 2° > 3° > 1°. The 3° suffers from steric hindrance to H-bonding with water (poor solvation of R3NH⁺).
  • Aliphatic amines are more basic than ammonia; alkyl groups donate electrons by +I, increasing electron density on N and stabilising the cation.

Aromatic amines (aniline) are much less basic than aliphatic amines or NH3 because the lone pair on N is delocalised into the ring (resonance), reducing its availability for H⁺. Anilinium ion has no such resonance.

Substituents on aniline: electron-donating groups (–OCH3, –CH3) at p- increase basicity slightly; electron-withdrawing (–NO2, –Cl) decrease basicity. p-nitroaniline is far less basic than aniline; the order is: p-toluidine > aniline > p-nitroaniline.

Comparative pKb (water): (CH3)2NH 3.27 < CH3NH2 3.38 < (CH3)3N 4.22 < NH3 4.75 < C6H5NH2 9.38.

sections.heading: Chemical Reactions of Amines
sections.body:
Alkylation: R2NH + R'X → R2NR' + HX. Continues to give quaternary salt with excess alkyl halide.

Acylation: 1° and 2° amines react with RCOCl, (RCO)2O or RCOOR' (in presence of base like pyridine to neutralise HCl) to give amides:
R–NH2 + R'COCl → R–NH–COR' + HCl. Acetylation of aniline → acetanilide (used to protect –NH2 in aniline before EAS).

Carbylamine reaction (isocyanide test): 1° amines (only) on heating with CHCl3 + alc. KOH give R–NC (isocyanide) with foul smell. Test for 1° amine.

Reaction with HNO2 (NaNO2 + dil. HCl, 0–5 °C):

  • 1° aliphatic amine: R–NH2 + HNO2 → R–OH + N2↑ + H2O (test: brisk N2 evolution).
  • 1° aromatic amine: Ar–NH2 + HNO2/HCl → Ar–N2⁺Cl⁻ (diazonium salt; stable at 0–5 °C). Key intermediate.
  • 2° amine (alkyl/aryl) → N-nitrosoamine (yellow oil): R2NH + HNO2 → R2N–N=O.
  • 3° amine (aliphatic): salt formation only; (Ar)(R)2N gives p-nitroso compound.

Hinsberg's test (with benzenesulphonyl chloride C6H5SO2Cl):

  • 1° amine: forms sulphonamide soluble in NaOH (acidic N–H).
  • 2° amine: forms sulphonamide insoluble in NaOH (no N–H).
  • 3° amine: no reaction (no replaceable H on N).

Electrophilic aromatic substitution on aniline: –NH2 is strongly activating and o/p-directing. Reactions:

  • Bromination: aniline + Br2/H2O → 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (white precipitate). To get monobromo, protect –NH2 as acetanilide first.
  • Nitration: in conc. HNO3/H2SO4 aniline gets protonated → m-directing → significant m-nitroaniline forms; for selective p-nitroaniline, acetylate aniline first.
  • Aniline does not undergo Friedel–Crafts (the AlCl3 coordinates to the amine lone pair, deactivating).

sections.heading: Diazonium Salts and Their Importance
sections.body:
Aromatic primary amines + HNO2 + HCl at 0–5 °C give arenediazonium salts Ar–N2⁺ X⁻. Stable in solution at low T; aliphatic diazonium salts are unstable, decomposing immediately to alcohols + N2.

Diazonium salts are extremely versatile synthons because –N2⁺ is a great leaving group (gives stable N2):

  • Ar–N2⁺ + CuCl/HCl → Ar–Cl (Sandmeyer); CuBr/HBr → Ar–Br; KI → Ar–I.
  • Ar–N2⁺ + Cu, HCl/HBr → Ar–Cl/Ar–Br (Gattermann).
  • Ar–N2⁺ + HBF4 → Ar–F (Balz–Schiemann).
  • Ar–N2⁺ + H2O, warm → Ar–OH + N2.
  • Ar–N2⁺ + H3PO2 + H2O → Ar–H + N2 (deamination, useful for removing –NH2 after using its directing power).
  • Ar–N2⁺ + CuCN/KCN → Ar–CN (Sandmeyer-cyanide).
  • Coupling reactions: Ar–N2⁺ + activated arene (phenol or aromatic amine) → azo compound Ar–N=N–Ar' (azo dyes; e.g. p-hydroxyazobenzene from PhN2⁺ + phenol in mildly alkaline conditions; coupling occurs at p-position of phenol/aniline).

Hence diazonium chemistry is the bridge between aromatic amines and an enormous variety of substituted arenes that cannot be made by direct substitution.

summary: Amines (R–NH_2, R_2NH, R_3N) are pyramidal at sp³ N; classified as 1°/2°/3° by H replacement and 4° as quaternary salt. Made by reduction of nitro (Sn/HCl), nitriles (LiAlH_4), or amides (LiAlH_4); ammonolysis of R–X (mixture); and degradative routes giving exclusively 1° amines: Gabriel phthalimide (no C lost) and Hofmann bromamide (one C lost). Basicity: aliphatic amines stronger than NH_3 (+I), with 2° > 1° > 3° in water (solvation); aromatic amines are much weaker (lone pair into ring). Key reactions: alkylation, acylation (acetanilide), carbylamine test (1°), Hinsberg test (distinguishes 1°/2°/3°), reactions with HNO_2 (aliphatic 1° → ROH + N_2; aromatic 1° → diazonium salt at 0–5 °C). Diazonium salts undergo Sandmeyer/Gattermann/Balz–Schiemann/hydrolysis/deamination/coupling, opening synthetic routes to aryl halides, phenols, biaryl, and azo dyes.
pitfalls: Predicting basicity order purely from +I — in water, 3° amines are weaker than 2° due to steric hindrance to solvation.
Saying aniline is more basic than ammonia — it is much less basic.
Using Friedel–Crafts directly on aniline — the AlCl_3 coordinates to NH_2 and deactivates the ring.
Brominating aniline directly to get monobromoaniline — get tribromo. Protect with acetylation first.
Confusing Gabriel phthalimide (works only for aliphatic 1° amines, no Ar–X) with Hofmann bromamide (works for aliphatic and aromatic 1° amines).
Forgetting that aliphatic diazonium salts are unstable — react immediately with water.
Mixing up Sandmeyer (uses CuX) with Gattermann (uses Cu/HX).
Carbylamine test — works only for 1° amines, gives foul-smelling isocyanide.
